title: The Last Order of the Night
content:
The GPS had been trying to kill Wang Lei for three years now.

He knew this the way a soldier knows his rifle or a chef knows his knife. The little blue line on his phone screen had a sick sense of humor. It loved sending him down alleyways too narrow for his scooter. It adored dead ends that weren't on any map. On good nights, it took him to the right building, just the wrong floor.

But tonight, it had outdone itself.

Wang Lei pulled his electric scooter to a stop at the mouth of an alley that didn't exist twenty minutes ago. He'd been driving this route for two years. Delivered noodles to the old woman on the corner at least forty times. Watched the same three stray cats fight over fish bones behind the convenience store. He knew every crack in the sidewalk, every broken streetlight, every pothole that could swallow a tire whole.

This alley was new.

The buildings on either side leaned toward each other like old men sharing a secret. Strings of red lanterns hung between them, unlit but swaying in a wind that didn't reach the street behind him. The pavement looked wet, though it hadn't rained in three days.

Wang Lei checked his phone. The blue dot pulsed right at the end of the alley. Address: 88 Taiping Lane, Apartment 4B.

He'd never heard of Taiping Lane.

"Package for Li Wei," he muttered, reading from the order screen. "One spicy hotpot base, extra numbing peppercorns, two servings of beef tripe, and a bottle of baijiu that costs more than my rent."

The pay was good. Three hundred yuan for a single delivery, plus a guaranteed tip if it arrived before midnight. That kind of money meant someone was desperate, or rich, or both. Wang Lei didn't care which. His landlord cared even less, and rent was due in five days.

He kicked down the scooter's stand and grabbed the insulated bag from the back. The bag was warm. The hotpot base was still bubbling, somehow. He'd picked it up from a restaurant that didn't have a sign, run by a woman with eyes that seemed to look through him rather than at him. She'd handed him the bag and said, "Don't stop. No matter what you hear."

People said weird things all the time. Wang Lei had learned not to ask questions.

He walked into the alley.

The sound of the street cut off behind him like a door slamming shut. No more traffic. No more distant music from the night market. Just the scuff of his sneakers on wet pavement and the drip-drip-drip of water from somewhere above.

The red lanterns stayed dark, but the buildings on either side began to change. Windows that had been dark flickered with candlelight. Shadows moved behind them, shapes that didn't match the people they should belong to. A woman's laugh echoed from somewhere above, too high, too sharp.

Wang Lei kept walking.

He'd learned early in this job that some things weren't worth investigating. The drunk who ordered noodles at 3 AM from a phone that had been disconnected for five years. The apartment number that didn't exist, but the food always disappeared from the doorstep anyway. The old man at 7E who paid in coins that had Emperor Qianlong's face on them.

This was just another weird delivery. The pay was good. The customer was waiting.

The alley dead-ended at a wall. No door. No gate. Just old brick, covered in peeling red paint and plastered with paper talismans that fluttered in the still air.

Wang Lei's phone buzzed. He looked down.

*Customer has updated delivery instructions. Please proceed to the entrance.*

The screen glitched. Lines of static crawled across the map, and the blue dot jumped to a location that wasn't on any street. Then the GPS voice, the one he'd heard ten thousand times, spoke a single sentence:

"You have arrived."

The wall opened.

It didn't slide or swing or crumble. One moment it was solid brick, and the next it was a doorway filled with light so bright Wang Lei had to shield his eyes. Warm air poured through, smelling of peaches and incense and something electric, like the moment before a thunderstorm.

His phone screen went black.

"Ah, hell no," Wang Lei said.

But his feet were already moving. Three hundred yuan. Rent in five days. The tip. He'd walked through worse. Once, he'd delivered to a funeral home at midnight and the corpse had sat up to sign for it. This was fine. This was just a door in a wall. Nothing weird about that.

He stepped through.

The light swallowed him whole.

---

Wang Lei landed on his back, the insulated bag still clutched to his chest, the hotpot base miraculously unspilled. He lay there for a moment, staring up at a ceiling that wasn't a ceiling. Clouds moved overhead, shot through with veins of golden light. Columns of jade rose around him, carved with dragons that actually moved, their stone eyes tracking his fall.

"Delivery!"

The voice was a bellow, deep and annoyed. Wang Lei sat up and found himself surrounded by chaos.

He was in a sorting center. He recognized the setup immediately—the long tables, the stacks of packages, the workers moving between them with practiced efficiency. But this sorting center was the size of a football stadium, and every surface was either jade, gold, or carved from single blocks of crystal. Packages floated through the air on their own, stacking themselves by size and destination. Workers in matching red robes shouted orders at each other, their voices echoing off the impossible ceiling.

And standing over him, arms crossed, face the color of a storm cloud, was a man in an embroidered robe who looked like he'd been born angry.

"You're late," the man said.

Wang Lei checked his phone. Still dead. He checked his watch. 11:47 PM. He'd made it with thirteen minutes to spare.

"I'm early," he said.

"Early is late in the Heavenly Court." The man grabbed the insulated bag from his hands. "The Immortal Peach Banquet starts at midnight. The Kitchen God has been screaming for this hotpot base for three hours. Do you know what happens when the Kitchen God doesn't get his spicy broth?"

"I'm guessing it's not good."

"Correct. It's not good." The man ripped open the bag and sniffed the contents. His face softened, just slightly. "At least it's still hot. How did you manage that?"

Wang Lei stood up, brushing cloud dust off his jacket. "Insulated bag. Double-layered. Thermal reflective lining. Costs more than my scooter."

"Your what?"

"My—" Wang Lei stopped. He looked around the sorting center again. The floating packages. The jade columns with moving dragons. The workers in red robes who were definitely not human, their eyes glowing faintly gold, their movements too smooth to be natural.

"You're telling me this is heaven," he said.

"The Heavenly Court, yes." The man frowned at him. "Did no one explain your assignment?"

"My assignment was a delivery to 88 Taiping Lane. I don't deliver to heavens. I deliver to apartments. Sometimes offices. One time to a boat."

"You delivered to the Heavenly Court. The address changes based on who needs to find it." The man held out his hand. "I am Zhao Gongming, Minister of the Heavenly Distribution Bureau. You are our newest courier."

"I'm a delivery driver for Meituan."

"Not anymore."

Wang Lei looked at the packages stacked around him. Thousands of them. Maybe millions. They filled the sorting center from floor to ceiling, stacked in formations that defied gravity. He saw boxes labeled with characters he couldn't read, jars that glowed from within, scrolls tied with golden thread that hummed with energy.

"Where's my scooter?" he asked.

"Your what?"

"My scooter. Electric. White. Had a dent in the front from when I hit a pothole on Renmin Road."

Zhao Gongming blinked. "It's... in the arrivals courtyard. With the other earthly deliveries."

Wang Lei walked toward the exit. Or what he hoped was the exit. The sorting center seemed to stretch in all directions, its walls lost in clouds and golden light. But he'd learned to read buildings the way he read streets—find the flow, follow the movement, look for where people were going instead of where they were.

The workers in red robes parted around him. Some bowed. Others just stared. He ignored them all.

"Where are you going?" Zhao Gongming called after him.

"To get my scooter. Then I'm going home. I have another shift at 8 AM."

"You can't just leave. You've been conscripted."

Wang Lei kept walking. "I'm a civilian contractor. I have rights. Union protections. A contract that says I don't have to work in any location that doesn't have proper street addresses and working WiFi."

"This is heaven."

"Yeah, and I bet your delivery ratings are terrible."

He found the arrivals courtyard through a set of doors that opened onto a platform floating in empty sky. The courtyard was a flat expanse of white marble, surrounded by pillars that held up nothing. Packages materialized in bursts of light, landing in neat rows before being sorted by floating carts that moved with mechanical precision.

And there, parked between a crate of glowing peaches and a cage full of chirping birds, was his scooter.

It looked exactly the same. The dent in the front. The cracked mirror he'd been meaning to fix for six months. The sticker on the side that said "I Brake for Noodles." The battery light was green, which meant it still had charge. In heaven. Where nothing made sense.

Wang Lei walked over and swung his leg over the seat. The scooter hummed to life under him, its electric motor whirring with familiar comfort.

"What are you doing?" Zhao Gongming had followed him. He stood at the edge of the courtyard, his embroidered robes flapping in a wind that didn't touch anything else.

"Going home." Wang Lei pulled up his phone. Still dead. But he'd memorized the route back. Fourteen turns, two lefts through the alley, one right at the broken streetlight. Simple.

"You can't go back through the same door."

"Watch me."

He twisted the throttle. The scooter lurched forward, and Wang Lei aimed for the set of doors he'd come through. The Cloudy Gates, according to the gold lettering above them. He'd go back through the sorting center, find the alley, and never think about heaven again.

The doors slammed shut before he reached them.

Wang Lei skidded to a stop. The doors were solid jade now, thirty feet high, engraved with characters that burned with inner fire. No handles. No hinges. Just wall.

Zhao Gongming walked up beside him. "The Heavenly Court doesn't let people leave easily. Especially not people who've seen the sorting center."

"I didn't see anything. I delivered hotpot. That's all."

"You saw the packages. The distribution routes. The logistics." Zhao Gongming's voice softened, taking on an almost pleading tone. "We have a problem, Wang Lei. A serious one. The Immortal Peach Banquet is in thirteen minutes, and the peaches haven't arrived."

"So send someone to get them."

"We've sent everyone. Three couriers didn't come back. Two cultivators got eaten. One celestial general resigned and joined the demon sect."

Wang Lei stared at him. "You want me to deliver peaches. Through demon-infested territory."

"Your rating is 99.7% on-time delivery."

"That was in Chengdu. Chengdu doesn't have demons."

"It has traffic."

Wang Lei had no answer to that. He sat on his scooter

title: The First Delivery Is Always the Hardest
content:
The Heavenly Court smelled like incense and old paperwork.

Wang Lei stood in the middle of what looked like a government office designed by someone who'd never seen a government office. Jade pillars rose to a ceiling that showed swirling clouds and occasional flashes of lightning. Desks made of polished white stone floated three inches off the ground. The air hummed with a low vibration that made his teeth itch.

His scooter sat beside him, tires still warm from the road that apparently led straight to heaven. The delivery bag hung from the handlebars, one immortality peach still tucked inside.

"Hey," he said to no one in particular. "Anyone got a return address?"

A woman looked up from behind a floating desk. She wore a flowing robe the color of dried blood, her black hair pinned up with what looked like actual chopsticks made of bone. Her face said she'd been dealing with other people's problems for about three thousand years too long.

"Name?" She didn't bother to stand.

"Wang Lei. I'm a delivery driver. I think I took a wrong turn."

"You think." She picked up a scroll, unrolled it with a flick of her wrist. "You're on the delivery manifest. One unit of immortality peach, grade seven, destination Jade Pool Pavilion. You're late."

"Late? I just got here."

"Your ETA was seventeen minutes ago." She tapped the scroll. "The system doesn't lie."

Wang Lei stared at her. Then at the scroll. Then back at her.

"I fell through a hole in reality," he said slowly. "My scooter almost landed on some kind of dragon. I'm pretty sure I passed a floating mountain on the way down. And you're telling me I'm late?"

"The system doesn't care about excuses."

He wanted to argue. He wanted to grab her by those bone chopsticks and explain exactly how stupid this whole situation was. But something stopped him. Something in the way she said system. In the way her eyes flicked to the peach in his bag.

He'd been a delivery driver for seven years. He knew when a customer was lying about being home. He knew when a package was going to be a problem. And he knew, with absolute certainty, that this woman wanted him to fail.

"Fine," he said. "Where's Jade Pool Pavilion?"

"Third celestial tier. Take the Cloud Bridge east, pass the Hall of Supreme Harmony, take the second left after the Peach Garden. Can't miss it."

"Great."

He didn't move.

She didn't blink.

"You're still here," she said.

"I need a map."

"Use your phone."

"My phone doesn't have service. I checked. No bars. No Wi-Fi. Nothing."

She sighed like he'd asked her to carry the package herself. "Third celestial tier. Take the Cloud Bridge east. Pass the Hall of Supreme Harmony. Take the second left after the Peach Garden. Can't miss it."

"You said that already."

"Then go."

Wang Lei looked at his scooter. The tires were standard issue. The battery had about thirty percent charge. The GPS was a black screen with the words No Signal bouncing around in the corner.

He'd delivered packages in typhoons. In traffic jams that lasted four hours. To customers who lived in buildings with no elevators and seventeen floors of stairs. He'd once hand-delivered a wedding cake to a couple who got married on a moving train.

This was just another delivery.

He got on the scooter. Turned the key. The engine hummed to life, a sound so ordinary it felt like a lie.

"Which way is east?" he asked.

The woman pointed without looking up.

He drove.

---

The Cloud Bridge was not a bridge.

It was a road made of actual clouds, stretching across an empty sky that dropped into nothing on both sides. Wang Lei kept his eyes straight ahead and his hands tight on the handlebars. The scooter's tires left faint impressions in the white surface, like driving on packed snow that wasn't cold.

Other beings passed him. Some rode clouds like surfboards. Some walked on air. One old man with a beard that touched his knees zipped past on what looked like a flying broom, but with more sparkles and fewer bristles.

No one looked at him twice.

That was fine. He didn't want to be looked at. He wanted to deliver the peach, find the return address, and get back to Chengdu before his shift ended. His boss would kill him if he lost another scooter.

The Hall of Supreme Harmony appeared on his left. It was exactly as impressive as the name suggested. Golden roof. Red pillars. Doors that stood sixty feet tall and probably weighed more than his entire apartment building. A crowd of minor officials in green robes streamed in and out, carrying scrolls and tablets and what looked like a giant abacus.

Wang Lei kept driving.

The Peach Garden came into view. He could smell it before he saw it — sweet and ripe and somehow ancient, like fruit that had been growing since before the first human took a bite of an apple. The trees stretched for miles, each one heavy with peaches that glowed faintly in the celestial light.

Second left.

He turned.

And found himself facing a wall of thorns.

They rose forty feet high, woven together like barbed wire designed by a god who really hated trespassers. The thorns were black. The vines were thick as his arm. And in the center, where a gate should have been, there was a face.

It was carved from the wood of the vines, roughly human, with eyes that opened and fixed on him.

"Delivery," the face said. Its voice was dry leaves and cracking bark.

"Yeah. For Jade Pool Pavilion."

"Wrong way."

"The lady at the front desk said—"

"The lady at the front desk wants you to fail."

Wang Lei stopped. Looked at the face. Looked back the way he'd come.

"What?"

"She sends every new courier here. The thorns eat them. She gets a bonus for lost packages." The face's mouth didn't move, but the words kept coming. "I've been watching for three hundred years. You're the first one who didn't try to force the gate."

"I'm a delivery driver. I don't force anything. I find a way."

The face considered this. Its wooden eyes creaked as they studied him.

"Interesting."

"Where's the real entrance?"

"There is no real entrance. The Jade Pool Pavilion doesn't exist."

Wang Lei felt his eye twitch. "What?"

"It's a dummy address. Used to catch couriers who don't know the system. The package gets 'lost' in transit, and the dispatcher keeps the contents." The face paused. "You have a grade seven immortality peach in that bag. Worth a fortune on the black market."

He looked down at the peach. It sat in his delivery bag, innocent and glowing.

"So what do I do?"

"You could give it to me."

"No."

"That was a test." The face almost smiled. "You passed. Now listen. The dispatcher you met at the front desk — her name is Zhao. She runs the low-tier courier division. She's been skimming packages for decades. Everyone knows. No one can prove."

"Prove what?"

"That the packages never arrive. That she's the one stealing them. That she's using the cult—"

The face stopped. Its eyes went wide.

"What cult?"

"Too late." The face's voice dropped to a whisper. "She knows I'm talking. Go. Now. Turn around, take the third left past the Hall of Supreme Harmony, and deliver to the real address. The one on the bottom of the manifest."

"There's nothing on the bottom of the manifest."

"Look again."

Wang Lei pulled the scroll from his bag. Unrolled it. At the very bottom, in handwriting so small he'd missed it, there was a second address.

Xiwangmu's Private Residence. Celestial Tier One. No Public Access.

"The Queen Mother of the West?" he said. "This is for her?"

"Direct order. Zhao was supposed to deliver it three weeks ago. She's been claiming the peaches are spoiled. The Queen Mother has been patient. Her patience is running out."

Wang Lei looked at the address. Looked at the peach. Looked at the face.

"Why are you helping me?"

The face didn't answer for a long moment. When it did, its voice was barely a rustle.

"Because Zhao killed my son. He was a courier too. He tried to expose her. She sent him to the thorns."

The vines trembled. Somewhere in the distance, a bell began to ring.

"She's coming," the face said. "You have five minutes. Deliver the peach. Expose her. Or die trying."

The vines parted, revealing a path that hadn't been there before.

Wang Lei didn't think. He turned the throttle and drove.

---

The path twisted through the Peach Garden, past trees that whispered in languages he didn't understand. The scooter's battery dropped to twenty percent. Then fifteen. The celestial air was thin, and the engine was struggling.

But he kept going.

The path ended at a wall of white jade. No gate. No door. Just a smooth surface that reflected his own terrified face back at him.

"Delivery!" he shouted.

Nothing.

"Delivery for Xiwangmu!"

Still nothing.

He looked at the peach. At the wall. At his scooter, which was down to ten percent battery.

Then he noticed something. A small indentation in the jade, about the size of a peach.

He pressed the fruit against it.

The wall slid open.

Inside, a woman sat on a throne made of clouds. She was beautiful in the way that mountains are beautiful — terrifying and eternal and completely indifferent to whether you lived or died.

"You're late," she said.

Wang Lei held up the peach.

"Traffic."

She looked at him. At the peach. At the sweat on his forehead and the fear in his eyes.

"Zhao sent you to the thorns."

"Yes."

"And you survived."

"She's been stealing from you. For decades. She's using the deliveries to fund something. A cult. I don't know what."

Xiwangmu's expression didn't change. But the temperature in the room dropped ten degrees.

"A cult," she repeated.

"That's what the face in the thorns said. Before she came."

"Zhao is here now. She's at the Hall of Supreme Harmony, filing a report that you stole the peach."

"I didn't—"

"I know." Xiwangmu stood. The clouds around her throne darkened. "But she doesn't know that I know. And that gives us an opportunity."

She walked toward him. Her feet didn't touch the ground.

"You want to go home, delivery driver?"

"Yes."

"Then help me catch a thief. And maybe, just maybe, I'll let you."

Wang Lei looked at his scooter. His dead phone. His empty delivery bag.

"What do I have to do?"

Xiwangmu smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Make a delivery. To Zhao."

The peach in his hand began to glow.

title: The Jade Emperor's Late Fee
content:
The celestial waiting room smelled like sandalwood and ozone.

Wang Lei sat on a cushion that probably cost more than his entire apartment back in Chengdu, watching a minor god in blue robes shuffle papers behind a desk made of solid jade. His scooter was parked outside, chained to a railing that looked older than Chinese civilization itself. The chain was his own, bought from a 7-Eleven in Wuhou District.

"You're certain you have no cultivation base?" the blue-robed god asked without looking up.

"I drive a scooter for a living."

"Mm." The god stamped something. "No spiritual roots?"

"I don't even know what that means."

"Qi circulation?"

"I'm not a doctor."

The god finally looked up. His face was smooth like porcelain, his eyes holding the weary patience of someone who'd been dealing with mortals for several thousand years. "How did you die?"

"I didn't." Wang Lei pulled out his phone. Dead. No signal. No wifi. "I was making a delivery. Now I'm here."

"You died during the delivery."

"I didn't die. I drove through a tunnel and ended up in a peach orchard owned by some woman who wanted to kill me until I mentioned I was delivering food."

The god's hand stopped moving. "You met the Queen Mother of the West?"

"Old lady with a lot of peach trees? Yeah. She ate my customer's order."

Silence filled the room. The god set down his brush and stood, his robes whispering against the floor. "Wait here."

He left through a side door that shimmered like heat haze.

Wang Lei waited.

He counted the cracks in the ceiling. He examined a vase that had dragons breathing actual fire around its rim. He watched a servant walk past carrying a tray of what looked like glowing dumplings. His stomach growled. He hadn't eaten since lunch, and that was just instant noodles from the convenience store near his regular pickup spot.

Twenty minutes passed. Or an hour. Time moved weird here, like it was taking a nap between shifts.

The door shimmered again. The blue-robed god returned, his face unreadable.

"The Jade Emperor will see you."

---

The throne room made the airport in Chengdu look like a bus station.

Columns stretched up into clouds. The floor was polished obsidian that reflected the stars above — except the ceiling was open to actual space, constellations rotating slowly like a planetarium on drugs. Musicians played instruments Wang Lei had never seen, producing music that vibrated in his bones.

And at the far end, on a raised platform that seemed to float, sat the Jade Emperor.

He looked exactly like every painting Wang Lei had ever seen. Long beard. Ornate robes. A hat with beads hanging from the front. But the eyes — those were wrong. Paintings never captured the eyes. They were old. Not old like a person, but old like a mountain. Like they'd watched civilizations sprout and crumble like morning glory.

"Delivery driver Wang Lei," the Jade Emperor said. His voice wasn't loud, but it filled the room completely. "You've caused quite a stir."

"I didn't mean to, Your Majesty. I just want to go home."

"The Queen Mother of the West speaks highly of you. She says you refused a bribe."

"She tried to give me a peach that would make me immortal. I told her I had other deliveries."

Laughter rippled through the court. The Jade Emperor's lips twitched.

"You understand the gravity of what you're refusing, mortal? That peach would grant you a place among the gods. Eternal life. Power beyond imagination."

Wang Lei thought about it. Really thought about it.

"I have a mother in Chengdu," he said. "She's sixty-two. She has high blood pressure. If I don't come home, she'll kill me. And not with immortality peaches."

More laughter. A goddess in flowing green robes covered her mouth. A muscular god with a spear slapped his knee.

The Jade Emperor raised one finger. The laughter stopped instantly.

"Your loyalty is noted. But there is the matter of your presence here. Mortals do not simply walk into the Heavenly Court. You entered through a breach in the celestial barrier, likely caused by improper maintenance of the southern dimensional seams."

"Sounds like a landlord problem."

"It is a god problem." The Jade Emperor leaned forward. "And I am assigning it to you."

Wang Lei's mouth opened. Closed. Opened again.

"Your Majesty, I'm a delivery driver. I don't know anything about dimensional seams or celestial barriers. I can't even change a tire."

"You have a 99.7% on-time delivery rating."

"That's different."

"Is it?" The Jade Emperor snapped his fingers. A scroll appeared in front of Wang Lei, floating at eye level. "You deliver a package. You overcome obstacles. You reach your destination. The skills transfer."

"This is insane."

"Perhaps." The Jade Emperor smiled, and it was terrifying. "But here is the situation, Wang Lei. The breach you entered through is unstable. Every day it remains open, demonic energy seeps into the mortal realm. Already, three villages in Sichuan have reported crops dying. Two have seen livestock born with two heads."

"That's not my fault."

"Nor is it mine. But you are the one who can fix it."

Wang Lei stared at the scroll. Characters glowed on its surface, shifting and reforming as he watched.

"What exactly would I have to do?"

"You will deliver packages to the guardians of the four celestial gates. Each guardian holds a seal that must be renewed. Once all four seals are restored, the breach will close."

"Deliver packages."

"Yes."

"To four gates."

"Yes."

"That's it?"

The Jade Emperor's smile widened. "The Southern Gate is guarded by a dragon who has not slept in three thousand years. The Western Gate sits in the middle of a battlefield where the souls of fallen soldiers fight an eternal war. The Northern Gate is frozen solid, home to a frost demon who consumes any living thing that approaches. And the Eastern Gate — " He paused. "The Eastern Gate is currently occupied by a rogue cultivator who believes he should be emperor."

Wang Lei felt something in his chest sink. It felt like his 99.7% rating was about to take a hit.

"I don't have a weapon," he said.

"You have a scooter."

"It's electric. I don't know if it even works here."

"It does. We've been charging it." The Jade Emperor gestured, and two minor gods wheeled in Wang Lei's scooter. It looked exactly the same, except now it glowed faintly with golden light. "We've also installed a celestial battery. It will never run out of charge in any realm."

"That's... actually pretty useful."

"The first package is ready." A servant approached, carrying a box wrapped in silk. It was about the size of a shoebox, light enough that Wang Lei could hold it with one hand. "Deliver this to the guardian of the Southern Gate. Do not open it. Do not lose it. Do not be late."

Wang Lei took the package. It felt warm, like it had been sitting in the sun.

"Your Majesty, I have a question."

"Speak."

"What's my rating going to be for this?"

The court went silent. The Jade Emperor blinked, clearly not expecting the question.

"Your rating?"

"Yeah. I get rated after every delivery. Bad ratings hurt my standing. If I'm going to work for you, I need to know what the performance metrics are."

The Jade Emperor stared at him for a long moment. Then he laughed. It started as a chuckle and grew into something that shook the columns and made the musicians stop playing.

"Performance metrics," he said, wiping his eyes. "I've ruled the heavens for ten thousand years, and no one has ever asked me about performance metrics."

"I'm a professional."

"Very well, Wang Lei." The Jade Emperor sat back, still smiling. "You will be rated on speed, condition of package, and customer satisfaction. If you succeed, you will be allowed to return to your mortal life. If you fail..." He gestured vaguely. "The consequences are unpleasant."

"What kind of unpleasant?"

"The kind that involves spending eternity as a minor functionary in the Department of Paperwork Processing."

Wang Lei shuddered. He'd seen the stacks of papers in that waiting room.

"I'll take the delivery."

"Good." The Jade Emperor waved his hand. "You leave immediately. The Southern Gate is three realms away. You'll need to pass through the Market of Forgotten Things and the River of Endless Rain. Do not stop at either."

"How long do I have?"

"Until the next full moon. That's when the breach widens. If it widens enough, the demonic energy will flood the mortal realm and everyone you know will die."

Wang Lei tucked the package under his arm. "No pressure."

He walked toward his scooter, the gods parting to let him through. As he reached the exit, a hand grabbed his shoulder.

He turned. It was the blue-robed god from the waiting room.

"One piece of advice, delivery driver."

"What?"

"Don't trust anyone who offers you a shortcut."

Wang Lei stared at him. The god's face was unreadable.

"Thanks," Wang Lei said. "I'll keep that in mind."

He climbed onto his scooter. The engine hummed to life, the golden glow brightening. A path opened before him, shimmering like a heat mirage, leading out of the Heavenly Court and into unknown territory.

He twisted the throttle.

The scooter shot forward, faster than it had ever moved on Earth. The wind screamed past his ears. Buildings of jade and gold blurred into streaks of light. He passed through a gate that felt like breaking the surface of water, and suddenly he was somewhere else.

A marketplace stretched before him, infinite and chaotic. Stalls sold shadows in jars. Merchants haggled over memories. Creatures that were half bird, half human, and all business shouted prices for things Wang Lei couldn't identify.

The Market of Forgotten Things.

He kept his head down and drove.

Stall after stall passed. A woman tried to sell him a box of regrets. A man offered to trade his shadow for Wang Lei's future. A child with empty eye sockets tugged at his sleeve, asking if he had any dreams to spare.

Wang Lei accelerated.

The market seemed endless. Every time he thought he'd reached the edge, more stalls appeared. More merchants. More creatures reaching for him with hands that left cold trails on his skin.

His phone buzzed.

He almost crashed.

It was still dead. No signal. No battery. But it was buzzing in his pocket like an alarm clock.

He pulled it out. The screen was black except for one line of text:

*You have a new order.*

*Destination: Southern Gate.*

*Deadline: 14 days, 6 hours, 32 minutes.*

*Customer: The Jade Emperor.*

*Rating: Pending.*

*Accept? Y/N*

Wang Lei stared at the screen. His phone had become a delivery app for gods.

He pressed Y.

The screen went black again. But now, a tiny map appeared in the corner, showing his current location and a route through the market to an exit he hadn't seen before.

He followed it.

The market ended abruptly, replaced by a river that stretched to the horizon. The water was falling upward. Droplets rose from the surface like reverse rain, climbing into clouds that rained back down, creating a cycle of water that went nowhere.

The River of Endless Rain.

Wang Lei looked at the map. The route went straight through.

"You've got to be kidding me."

He revved the throttle and drove onto the surface of the river.

The scooter floated. The wheels spun against the water like it was solid ground. Rain fell upward and sideways, soaking him instantly

title: The Immortal Grudge List
content:
The Eastern Kitchen of the Heavenly Court smelled like a thousand restaurants fighting each other.

Wang Lei's nose caught soy sauce, sesame oil, chili crisp, and something else—something that burned all the way down to his lungs. He stood at the edge of a kitchen that stretched further than his eyes could follow. Bronze woks the size of car tires hung from hooks. Steam rose from cauldrons that bubbled with broths that probably cost more than his apartment.

A woman in a stained apron pointed a cleaver at him.

"You're the mortal?"

Her voice cut through the kitchen noise like she was used to being obeyed. Gray streaked her hair, pulled back in a bun so tight it pulled her eyes upward. Her arms were thick as hams. The cleaver in her hand had dents along the blade—not from age. From use.

Wang Lei held up the jade token. "Delivery. For someone named—"

"I know what it is." She snatched it from his hand. Her fingers were calloused, nails short and practical. She turned the token over, grunted, and threw it into a drawer full of identical tokens. "Fourth one this week. That lazy bastard in the Southern Gate is losing them again."

"I don't think it was lost. I brought it straight from—"

"You brought it." She pointed the cleaver at a stool in the corner. "Sit. Eat. You look like you're about to fall over."

Wang Lei's legs made the decision before his brain did. He sat. The stool was lower than he expected, and he almost tipped backward. By the time he'd caught his balance, a bowl had appeared in front of him. Noodles. Broth. A piece of braised pork belly the size of his fist.

"Eat," she said. "Then tell me why you're really here."

He picked up the chopsticks. The first bite hit his tongue and he forgot how to breathe. The pork belly melted. The broth tasted like every good memory he'd ever had about food, concentrated into a single mouthful. He ate without thinking, without stopping, until the bowl was empty and he was licking the last drops from his lips.

"Good?" The woman was watching him from across a counter, wiping her cleaver with a rag.

"I think I just had a religious experience."

She laughed. It was a rough sound, like gravel rolling downhill. "First good thing I've heard all day. I'm Granny Jiang. Head cook of the Eastern Kitchen. You can call me Granny."

"Wang Lei. Delivery driver."

"I know who you are. Word travels fast in heaven when a mortal shows up with a scooter." She hung the cleaver on a hook. "The other gods are laughing about it. 'Look at the little delivery boy, running around the Celestial Realm like he owns it.'"

"I don't own anything. I just want to go home."

"Home." Granny Jiang's eyes narrowed. "You think home still exists the way you left it?"

The words hit harder than they should have. Wang Lei's hands tightened on the empty bowl. "What do you mean?"

"I mean time moves different here. You've been in heaven for—" she glanced at a wall clock that had no numbers, only symbols he didn't recognize "—about six hours. Down there? Two weeks."

Two weeks. His mother would have called the police. His landlord would have thrown his stuff on the street. The delivery company would have fired him and blacklisted his name across every platform in Chengdu.

"Can I go back?"

"You can." Granny Jiang poured herself a cup of tea from a pot that steamed despite having no fire under it. "But the door you came through? That was a one-way trip. The Heavenly Court doesn't let mortals wander in and out. You'd need a guide. Someone with clearance to open the gates."

"Who has that?"

She sipped her tea. Didn't answer.

"Granny?"

"The Jade Emperor. The Four Heavenly Kings. The Queen Mother." She set down her cup. "And every department head with gate access. But they won't help you for free. Nothing in heaven is free, boy. You want a favor, you earn it."

Wang Lei's phone buzzed. He pulled it out—the screen was cracked now, the battery at 12%. A notification had appeared, floating above the normal icons.

**NEW ORDER: Celestial Courier Contract**

**Client: Eastern Kitchen (Granny Jiang)**

**Package: Emergency spice delivery to the Southern Bastion**

**Distance: 47 celestial li**

**Time Limit: Before midnight bell**

**Reward: Gate access token + 500 merit points**

**Accept? [YES] [NO]**

He looked up. Granny Jiang was smiling.

"The system works fast up here," she said. "I put in the request the moment you sat down."

"You could have just asked."

"Where's the fun in that?" She reached under the counter and pulled out a cloth bag, tied with red string. Inside, something smelled like pepper and lightning. "This is for the Southern Bastion. It's a fortress at the edge of demon territory. The garrison commander there has been waiting for this shipment for three months. His soldiers are getting restless."

Wang Lei looked at the bag. Then at his phone. Then at Granny Jiang.

"Why me? You've got immortals. You've got flying horses. I've got a scooter with a dying battery."

"Because the immortals are lazy. The flying horses are stubborn. And you—" she pointed at him "—you have a 99.7% on-time delivery rating. I checked."

"You can check that?"

"Your phone is connected to the Heavenly Network now. Everything you've ever done as a courier is public record." She leaned forward. "Do you know how rare a 99.7% rating is? Even the Celestial Postal Service can't manage that. They lose half their packages to cloud dragons."

Wang Lei stared at his phone. The notification pulsed, waiting for his answer.

"One delivery," he said. "Then you give me the gate token."

"One delivery. But I'm adding a bonus condition." Granny Jiang's smile sharpened. "While you're at the Southern Bastion, I want you to ask about a package. A jade box, about this big." She held her hands apart, showing something the size of a shoebox. "It was supposed to arrive at the Eastern Kitchen six months ago. It never did."

"Lost package?"

"Stolen." The word came out cold. "Someone in the Southern Bastion took it. I want to know who."

"You want me to investigate a theft. While delivering spices. On a scooter. In demon territory."

"You want to go home, don't you?"

Wang Lei pressed ACCEPT.

His phone pinged. The screen flashed bright white, then reset. The battery showed 100%. A new map appeared, showing a route that snaked through clouds and mountain peaks, marked with warnings in red text.

**Navigation activated. Estimated arrival: 3 hours.**

**Warning: Route passes through contested territory.**

**Warning: Demon activity detected along secondary roads.**

**Warning: Do not engage. Do not stop. Do not make eye contact.**

"Great advice," Wang Lei muttered. "Very specific."

Granny Jiang handed him the spice bag. It was heavier than it looked, warm to the touch. "The garrison commander's name is General Zhao. He's a hard man. Drinks too much. Fights too much. But he's fair. If you deliver this on time, he'll remember it."

"And the jade box?"

"Ask around. Quietly. Don't let anyone know what you're looking for." She grabbed his shoulder. Her grip was iron. "One more thing. The Southern Bastion is the last stop before the demon lands. Things get strange out there. Laws are different. If something feels wrong—"

"It is wrong?"

"Trust your gut. It's kept you alive this long."

Wang Lei left the Eastern Kitchen with the spice bag slung across his back, the weight settling against his spine like a second spine made of responsibility and regret.

His scooter was where he'd left it, parked between two marble pillars. Someone had attached a red ribbon to the handlebars. A good luck charm. He didn't know who'd put it there, but he wasn't about to take it off.

The engine coughed twice before catching. The headlights flickered, then steadied. The map on his phone showed a road that didn't exist in any world he'd ever known—a path of light cutting through darkness, marked with waypoints and warnings.

He twisted the throttle.

The scooter lurched forward, picked up speed, and then the ground fell away.

Wang Lei was flying.

Not flying like a bird. Flying like a rock thrown from a slingshot. The scooter shot across a bridge of clouds, wheels spinning against nothing but air. Wind screamed past his ears. Below him, mountains stabbed upward like broken teeth. Above him, stars spun in patterns that made his eyes hurt.

"Okay," he said to no one. "Okay. This is fine."

The road curved. He leaned into it, the way he'd learned delivering noodles through Chengdu traffic. The scooter responded like it knew what he needed before he did. They banked around a peak that had a pagoda on top, its roof tiles glowing faint gold.

His phone buzzed.

**New message from: Granny Jiang**

**"Forgot to mention. The demons have been attacking delivery routes lately. They're hungry. Desperate. Something's changed in the wastelands."**

**"Don't die. I just got a good delivery driver."**

Wang Lei laughed. It came out shaky.

The clouds ahead of him parted, and he saw it: the Southern Bastion.

It wasn't a building. It was a mountain that had been hollowed out and fortified. Walls of black stone rose from the earth, lined with glowing runes. Watchtowers pierced the sky. Between them, a gate made of what looked like solid jade stood closed, its surface carved with dragons and tigers fighting each other.

He was still a kilometer away when the arrows started flying.

The first one missed by a meter. The second one clipped his rearview mirror. The third one—

Wang Lei slammed the brakes.

The scooter skidded to a stop on a cloud platform that materialized beneath his wheels. He raised his hands. The arrows stopped.

A voice boomed from the walls.

"State your business, mortal."

"I'm a delivery driver!"

Silence. Then laughter. Rough, ugly laughter from multiple throats.

"We don't get many delivery drivers out here," the voice said. "Especially not living ones."

"I have a package for General Zhao. From the Eastern Kitchen."

The laughter stopped.

The jade gate groaned. Started to open.

But before it had moved even a crack, something else happened. A shadow fell over Wang Lei. Something blotted out the stars. He looked up.

A figure stood on a cloud above him. A woman in armor that gleamed like liquid silver. Her hair was white as snow, her eyes were gold, and she held a spear that crackled with electricity.

"Wang Lei," she said. Her voice was thunder wrapped in silk. "I've been looking for you."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Princess Iron Fan. And you—" she pointed the spear at his chest "—have something that belongs to me."

Wang Lei's hand went to the spice bag. Then to his pocket, where the jade token from his first delivery still sat.

"Three deliveries," he said. "I've made three deliveries. Which one are you talking about?"

Princess Iron Fan smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"All

title: The Jade Emperor's Late Fee
content:
Wang Lei's scooter rattled to a stop in front of the Southern Heavenly Gate, and for the first time in six hours, he allowed himself to breathe.

The gate guards didn't even look up anymore. One of them—the shorter one with the tiger-skin kilt—just waved him through with a lazy flick of his wrist. "Another peach delivery?"

"Document pouch for the Star of Literature," Wang Lei said, killing the engine. "Signature required."

The guard snorted. "Since when does Wenchang need pouches delivered? Man's got a qilin that can cross the sky in three breaths."

"Not my problem. I just drive."

He'd been in the Heavenly Court for three full days now. Three days of navigating marble halls that rearranged themselves when he wasn't looking, of dodging celestial officials who walked through walls, of learning that the Cloud Department's internal mail system was somehow both magical and worse than any human bureaucracy he'd ever dealt with.

Three days of making deliveries.

It should have been impossible. He had no cultivation. No spiritual power. No technique that would let him breathe in the void between realms or survive the miasma that surrounded demon territory. But the gods didn't need him for any of that. They needed him for what he could do on the ground. In the city. In the mortal world.

Because apparently, even gods had to deal with paperwork.

Wang Lei kick-started his scooter—the ancient battery light blinking weakly—and threaded through the morning crowd of minor deities and celestial attendants. A pair of fox spirits in scholar robes hopped out of his way, clutching scrolls to their chests. A minor rain dragon coiled around a pillar, giving him a look that suggested it had never seen anything so undignified as a man on a two-wheeled vehicle.

He didn't care. The scooter was his lifeline. His only connection to the world he'd been ripped from. And as long as he could keep riding, keep delivering, keep moving, he could pretend this was just another shift.

A really, really weird shift.

The Star of Literature's mansion sat at the eastern edge of the Celestial Library complex, a sprawling structure of red lacquer and gold trim that looked like it had been designed by someone who'd never learned the word "subtle." Wang Lei parked next to a stone lion that was definitely watching him, grabbed the document pouch, and walked up the seventy-two steps.

He was halfway to the door when a voice stopped him cold.

"You're the mortal."

Wang Lei turned. A woman stood at the base of the steps, dressed in white robes that seemed to drink the light around them. Her face was beautiful in the way a blade is beautiful—sharp, precise, and dangerous. She held a jade flute in one hand, and her eyes were the color of old gold.

"I—" Wang Lei's throat went dry. "Yeah. That's me."

"I've heard about you." She walked up the steps, and the air grew colder with each step she took. "The delivery boy who broke through the Heavenly Veil. The mortal who rides a thunderless chariot. The one who isn't afraid."

"Who said I'm not afraid?"

"Everyone who's watched you work." She stopped two steps below him, forcing him to look down at her. "I'm Chang'e. I have a package that needs delivering."

Wang Lei's brain short-circuited. The moon goddess. The one who'd drunk the elixir and floated up to the heavens, leaving her husband behind. The one who lived alone in the Cold Palace with nothing but a jade rabbit for company.

"I... I don't do personal deliveries," he managed. "I'm assigned through the Celestial Courier Office. You have to submit a request form, get it approved by the Bureau of—"

"I know the bureaucracy." Chang'e's voice was flat. "And I know that you can bypass it. You've already delivered seven packages outside the official channels in the past three days."

Wang Lei's mouth opened. Closed. "How do you know that?"

"I watch." She stepped closer, close enough that he could smell frost and osmanthus flowers. "I watch everything, mortal. I've been watching the Heavenly Court for three thousand years. There's very little that escapes my notice. And right now, I need someone who can move without being seen."

"Why me?"

"Because you don't glow." She gestured at him. "Every immortal in this realm has a aura, a cultivation signature. You have nothing. You're invisible to anyone who looks with spiritual sight. And your scooter—" She almost smiled. "It makes no sound in the celestial winds. It's like you don't exist at all."

Wang Lei didn't know how to feel about that. On one hand, it meant he could move freely. On the other hand, it meant he was essentially a ghost in a city of gods.

"What's the package?"

Chang'e reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small box wrapped in silk. It was no bigger than a deck of cards, and it pulsed with a pale blue light that seemed to throb in time with his heartbeat.

"This is a memory," she said. "A mortal memory. My husband's."

"Your husband? Hou Yi?"

"Don't say his name." Her voice cracked, just slightly. "Not here. Not where the walls have ears."

Wang Lei looked at the box. Looked at her. The desperation in her eyes was real, raw, and completely at odds with her cold beauty.

"What do you want me to do with it?"

"Deliver it to him. To the mortal realm. He lives in seclusion now, in a valley to the east of Kunlun. The demons have been thick in that region since the barrier weakened, and no immortal courier will risk the journey. But you—" She met his eyes. "You're already mortal. The demons won't sense you. The barrier won't reject you. You can walk where we cannot."

"And if I say no?"

"Then I'll find another way." But her voice said she wouldn't. That there was no other way.

Wang Lei thought about it. About the job he'd been given—the one that would let him return home if he completed it. About the gods who treated him like a novelty, a pet, a tool. About the stack of overdue delivery notices in his pocket that the Bureau of Celestial Logistics kept piling on him.

This was different. This was a person—a goddess, sure, but a person—asking for help. Begging, almost.

"If I do this," he said slowly, "I need something in return."

"What?"

"Information. About the Heavenly Court. About how it works. About who's really in charge, and what they want from me." He held up a finger. "And a battery charger that actually works. My scooter's running on fumes."

Chang'e studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded, just once.

"Deal."

---

The delivery to Wenchang took less than ten minutes. The Star of Literature was a distracted old man with ink-stained fingers and a habit of talking to himself, and he signed for the document pouch without even looking at Wang Lei.

But as Wang Lei turned to leave, a voice stopped him.

"Boy."

He turned back. Wenchang was looking at him now, his eyes sharp and clear.

"There's a storm coming. Not the kind with rain and wind. The kind that breaks worlds." He tapped his pen on the desk. "I've seen it in the texts. The Jade Emperor is calling a council tonight. Five hours from now. You'll be summoned."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the variable. The unknown factor. The thing that wasn't supposed to exist in the equation." Wenchang smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "The gods are afraid, boy. They've been afraid for a long time. And nothing makes a god more dangerous than fear."

Wang Lei walked out of the mansion with his skin crawling.

The scooter started on the third kick, which was better than he'd expected. He made two more deliveries in the next hour—a bag of celestial herbs to the Medicine Pavilion, a sealed scroll to the Ministry of Seasonal Affairs—and tried not to think about what Wenchang had said.

But the words stuck. They burrowed under his skin and sat there, heavy and cold.

*Nothing makes a god more dangerous than fear.*

He was on his way back to the Courier Office when the sky changed.

One moment it was clear, the eternal twilight of the Heavenly Court casting long purple shadows across the marble streets. The next, a shadow fell over everything—a shadow that came from above, from something so large it blocked out the light.

Wang Lei looked up.

And his heart stopped.

The creature descending from the heavens was like nothing he'd ever seen. It had the body of a tiger, the wings of a bat, and scales that shimmered like oil on water. Its tail was a serpent, its claws were scythes, and its eyes—

Its eyes were human.

It landed in the square ahead of him with a crash that shattered stone. Immortals scattered, screaming. A pavilion collapsed. The ground trembled.

And the creature spoke.

"Wang Lei." Its voice was a thousand voices layered together, a chorus of the dead. "The Lord of the Eastern Wastes sends his regards. He wants you to know that your delivery record ends here."

Wang Lei's hand went to his scooter's handlebars. His heart hammered. His palms were slick with sweat.

"Look," he said, his voice steady despite everything, "I don't know who you are or what you want, but I'm on a schedule. I've got a 99.7% on-time rating, and I'm not losing it because some scaly bastard decided to block traffic."

The creature tilted its head. Confusion flickered in those human eyes.

"What?"

"You heard me." Wang Lei revved the scooter. "I don't do combat. I don't do cultivation. I do deliveries. And right now, I've got a package that needs to be at the Ministry of Seasonal Affairs in ten minutes."

The creature laughed. It was a horrible sound, like grinding bones.

"You think your mortal errands matter? You think your little packages mean anything against the power of the Eastern Wastes?"

"I think," Wang Lei said, "that you've never met a delivery driver on a deadline."

He twisted the throttle.

The scooter surged forward—not toward the creature, but past it, weaving between its legs with a precision born from three years of dodging Chengdu traffic. The creature swiped at him, but he was already gone, cutting through the shattered square and into the narrow alleys beyond.

Behind him, something roared.

And in front of him, the sky turned red.

He didn't stop. He couldn't stop. He drove through alleys that twisted like snakes, through streets that melted into staircases, through courtyards that opened into different courtyards. The Heavenly Court was a maze, and he was lost in it, but he kept moving, kept riding, kept—

His scooter died.

The battery light flickered once and went dark. The engine coughed. And Wang Lei rolled to a stop in front of a door he'd never seen before.

A door made of black jade.

A door carved with symbols that hurt to look at.

A door that was opening.

A woman stepped out. She wore robes of midnight and starlight, and her face was veiled in shadow. She held a scroll in one hand, and her voice was soft as silk over a blade.

"Wang Lei. Finally." She unrolled the scroll. "I have a delivery for you. The most important one you'll ever make."

She held it up.

And at the top of the scroll,
